does: This paper develops a framework for measuring how well reward models align with the opinions and perspectives of different demographic groups. The authors evaluate reward models trained on human preference data across controversial topics where genuine disagreement exists, quantifying the models' captured opinions, attitudes, and values. They demonstrate that reward models exhibit significant sociodemographic biases, systematically favor certain demographic perspectives while underrepresenting others, and can actively reward harmful stereotypes. The paper further shows that simple prompting-based steering mechanisms, while capable of shifting reward model behavior toward target groups, cannot fully correct these alignment problems, revealing fundamental limitations in how reward models aggregate and represent human preferences during training.
connects: This work extends the growing body of research on reward model reliability and human preference learning in language model alignment. It builds on prior investigations into biases in preference data and concerns about whose values are represented in RLHF training, while providing more systematic measurement tools. The paper connects to research on demographic bias in NLP systems and fairness in machine learning, applying these concerns specifically to the reward modeling stage of alignment. It also engages with literature on value pluralism and the difficulty of capturing diverse human preferences, responding to recent work questioning whether single reward models can adequately represent heterogeneous human values and preferences across populations.
enables: These findings suggest several important directions for future work on alignment and human preference learning. The paper motivates research into alternative architectures for preference learning that can better accommodate demographic diversity, such as ensemble or multi-model approaches that don't collapse diverse perspectives into a single reward signal. It points toward the need for more sophisticated methods for constructing training data that consciously represent underrepresented groups and perspectives. The work also opens questions about transparency and auditing of reward models before deployment, and how to design preference learning procedures that actively mitigate social bias propagation. More broadly, it enables discussions about whether reward models trained on general preference data are the right approach for alignment given persistent demographic disparities.
